speaker: interviewer
text: Irene Zisblatt, tape four. Irene, you were describing the early days in Birkenau and the death of your cousin, your mother's cousin.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's cousin.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your father's cousin.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were trying to keep her alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were trying to keep her alive.

speaker: interviewee
text: But she--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember when she died?

speaker: interviewee
text: She died just a couple of weeks after we were there, in the very early days. And her sister and I remained, and we tried to be together as much as we-- we could. We couldn't on the outside, and most of the time inside the barrack we couldn't show that we belong together, because they used to separate people that became close to one another. They immediately put them-- took them apart. So you don't have a relationship or support system, because we were being treated like we were nothings. Like, a bug in the mud was better off than we were. Every day at [INAUDIBLE] appell, we were out there in the rain and in the mud, and-- and people crying and starving and-- and being sick, being afraid to go to seek for help. Of course, there was no help, actually. And just constant, constant torture and misery. It was-- it was just-- most of us said we would be better off dead. And a lot of people did go to the barbed wire. There was electricity in the barbed wire. Every time there was a transport coming through, the electricity went up, and the-- the-- the barbed wires were electrified. So it was-- they-- they were controlling it, when it was on and when it was off. When it was on, people would walk up to it just to die. When they saw that too many people were taking their own lives, that didn't satisfy them too well, because they wanted them to die when they wanted them to die, and not when the people wanted them to die. So they said, for every person that's going to take their lives, they're going to torture to dead five of us. So, of course, everybody ignored the-- the barbed wire from then on in. Nobody died at the wire from that-- from that day on. But there were other ways. It was-- the bathroom, we had one bathroom for 32,000 people. [? Teilager ?] had 32 barracks that people lived in. They had more barracks that they used for storage and the kitchen and whatever. But 32,000 people went to one latrine, once a day. So you can imagine the torture and the agony to-- until you were able to go. And then the food you were given was meant to destroy us, not-- not to keep us alive. There was enough rations to keep us alive maybe for three months from the time of arrival until the time of death. Of course, thank God, a lot of us surprised them. And-- and so the-- the-- the torture of being alive was always there, every second, every minute, everywhere. But now, we were the two of us left and, we weren't even allowed to be showing each other support, for fear that somebody will notice it and then we'll be separated again. But we were separated anyway. She was taken away. There were selections every single day, and the people were selected to be taken for certain-- for certain things. See, we weren't tattooed-- I wasn't tattooed right away. I was tattooed after they selected me for something, because then we found out that when we came in, that they were-- the reason we weren't tattooed right away is they didn't want us to live.

speaker: interviewer
text: We were in that camp as reserves for the gas chamber, for the crematorium, because they were-- they were burning two men and one woman, because that was the best efficient thing to have the crematorium work efficiently. Because the woman has a little more flesh on the body, because of her breasts, and the men don't. So they were using two men and one-- two men and one woman for the crematorium. So we were reserved, is what we were told by our block [INAUDIBLE]. Because every time we asked for some food or rations, you think you're here to live? You are here to be a reserve for-- for the-- for that chimney.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's when we found out what that chimney was. We saw the flames and the smoke and the stench was unbelievable. And we didn't know what it was for a few days. And she said, your parents are in there. Your siblings are burned there. That's what that-- and oh, my God, after that, for sure, more people wanted to die. And-- and so every day, the selections were for different purposes. They took us, a lot of us, for the gas chamber, for that third person so the crematorium burns better. And a lot of us were lucky enough to be selected for work. I was selected for experiments. 15 of us, one morning, were selected. We had to undress outside, and we had-- and-- and this doctor, this German doctor examined our body. And with they were-- they took us out because we had no blemished bodies. They wanted-- they wanted people with smooth skin and no blemishes on the skin. And we were already in the camp about a month, and we were already like, ready-- we were losing weight, but we-- we, I guess, looked still OK. So they selected 15 of us for smooth skin. And-- Did you remember the name of the doctor? Did you ever hear it?

speaker: interviewer
text: No. It could have been Eichmann. It could have been Mengele. You know, after a while, they all look the same, and it didn't-- it wasn't important, you know, to us, what their names were. They were our enemy and we need-- we needed to be strong when they came around. We-- we pinched our cheeks, and we hit our neighbors so they'd look healthy. So when they look at us, so they don't take us into that chimney.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so I-- I, for one, wasn't interested in what their names were or where they came from. All I know is that they're not our friends. And we need to be very, very careful what we do and what we say to speed up the process of our death, if-- if we want to still live in this misery. And most of the time we didn't, but we did anyway. And so we were selected, 15 of us, for-- for smooth skin, and we didn't know what that meant. And nobody would-- we would ask our-- the block [INAUDIBLE], which she was Polish, and we don't-- I-- to the very day, I don't know whether she was Jewish or she was not Jewish, but she was-- she was in there for a long time. And if she was Jewish, then the reason she was mean is because she saw too much. And I forgive her for whatever she did. I-- I think she had a valid reason for being the way she was. But she-- even she wouldn't say where we're going or why they-- they selected us. And maybe she didn't know. I don't know. But they took us away and they put us in-- they took-- we walked to Auschwitz from Birkenau. Of course, at that time, Auschwitz was different then. When I went back on the March of the Living, it's-- it w it wasn't there. But the two were connected. All of them were connected. Buna and you know, but you just walked certain areas to-- they took you. And they took us to Auschwitz, and they put us in the dungeon. And the dungeon was underneath a barrack. And there were cubicles, I would say the size of this table, or maybe half the size of this table. They were sectioned off, like, and we were put in, in those little sections. And there was water on the-- on the floor, like, up to our ankles. And we were standing in it, and they closed the door and it was pitch dark. There was-- oh, they put drops in our eyes. Before they-- they put us in the dungeon, they put drops in our eyes. They closed the door, and it was pitch dark. And I don't know how long we were down there. It was 15 of us down there. Each cubicle, we were only able to go into the next cubicle, like so. Two people, occasionally you can touch or you can talk. And we were in there with no food, with no light. The water we stood in, we drank, we went to the bathroom in. And I don't know whether we were there five days or five weeks, or-- no idea of time. They opened the door at the end of this whatever-- we were under experiment and we didn't know. We didn't know why we were there. They tattooed-- they tattooed us then. When they took us-- when they selected us from the [INAUDIBLE] appell, they took us through this little section and they-- they gave us our number. They tattooed us, and then we went into the dungeon.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we had the arm. We didn't know why we were tattooed. And then we had-- we couldn't see. We couldn't-- we didn't eat. We didn't-- we just drank that water. And then they opened the door. The only day that the time I was in Birkenau I saw sunlight like that. The sun-- the most beautiful sunny day. I never saw it again there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they took us out, and they came up to each one of us and they asked us what we can see. And some of them-- some of them couldn't see at all. They were-- they were blind. And I saw daylight. I didn't see any outlines right away. So they looked at my eyes and everybody else's, and they took us back to our barrack. And later on we found out that they were trying-- they were using drops in our eyes to see whether the color of our eyes would change. That was the experiment. Maybe that's why my eyes are tearing today. I don't know. Then-- and then they didn't take us anywhere, but when I came back, my cousin was gone. They took her somewhere else. So I was left alone. The people that were in the experiment with me, I-- the-- the-- the girl that was in the-- in the dungeon, like, next to me, and I was able to touch her, or talk to her. And we didn't see each other, what we looked like while we were there. But when we came out, we were holding on to each other. And so we stood next to one another so we can see each other and see who we are. And so they brought her back to-- to Birkenau to the laager [INAUDIBLE], but she wasn't in my barrack. She was in a different barrack. And-- but I knew what she looked like, and she knew what I looked like. And then they took us for experiment again, but then there was only five of us. I don't know what happened to the other 10, and she was among the five.

speaker: interviewer
text: So every-- then we went for an experiment where they were injecting some kind of a serum under the nail of our finger and sent us back to the barrack. And then took us back to see what it does and took blood. And then we found out that they were doing some kind of a virus testing. And I had an infection going up from my-- that's why my finger is disordered like that, from the injection. And I had a red line going up my arm when they sent me back to the barrack, in the barrack.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I-- I remembered that-- from school that that means a blood infection. And the way to treat it is to tie it up. And of course, I had nothing to tie it up. And I couldn't-- see, I used my one piece of clothing for toilet paper. Every time I was able to get to the latrine or go anywhere I would tear off a piece of my clothing and use it as toilet paper.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was very careful what I was using my piece of clothing for. But I had to tear off a piece of string to tie up my arm. And I was afraid to tell anybody, because that was an instant crematorium trip, if something was wrong with you. So I was afraid to tell anybody. I tied up my arm in the middle of the night. And I went-- I took my life in my hands and I went out of the barrack to the back door. See, the barracks had two entrances. And the back-- the same doors. Only the back door very seldom opened, because they-- they had you going out one door all the time. But it was always a little bit-- it was never shut tight because it wasn't constructed that well. So I-- I went-- I went to the back door, and I didn't open the door, but I put my arm out through the door, through the crack of the door, into the mud on the-- on the ground. And I stayed there until daybreak. And then at daybreak I went to [INAUDIBLE] appell from there, and the line was down from the cold-- from the mud-- from the-- you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then that was gone. Because see, the next day, they took us back to see how it is. And if they would have seen that, they would have sent me to the gas chamber. So I was so thankful that I had no longer a red line going up my arm. And then they examined our- but my finger was damaged. See, my finger was-- the nail came off and my-- my whole finger was like a pretzel. It was like, leaning on this finger for years after I came out of the camp. And with-- with massaging and therapy and all of that, it-- it's-- it's OK, but it's still-- it'll never be what this one is.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so that was the second experiment that we lived through, the five of us. And this one girl, her name was Sabka, and she was Polish, and she came into Auschwitz in 1943. They caught her. She was in hiding, and they caught her, and they brought her into Auschwitz. You want me to tell you what happened to her? How she wound up, or just you want me to tell my relationship with her? Your relationship.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, well she came into Auschwitz in 1943, and in 1944, she was selected for the experiments, and we were together. But she was already-- she was six years older that I was. She was 19 years old when we met. And she was at Auschwitz. She was already in the war for six years, in hiding and in ghettos and then in-- in the camps. So she was a little bit more wiser to the situation. And so it was good, because she-- she was very-- she-- she-- she conditioned herself to the-- to the situation, and she was like my support. She was, like, helping me to deal with all these atrocities and the experiments, especially. They were the worst. And so when we got to the third experiment, we were-- we kind of-- we took the-- the chance of talking to each other a little bit more. And she said, it's OK. I think so. I think if they-- even if they find out that we are friends, they-- they going to keep us for this, whatever is coming up.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she was telling me her-- how she got in there and-- and she had nobody. They-- she was all alone too. And she was like my strength, helping me to cope with not having my family and not having my friends and not having my cousins and not having anybody that looks familiar. So she says, this is how I'm dealing with-- with the situation. And you have to-- I'll never forget, she said, you have to be very strong. You have to close your eyes when you see somebody suffering and just say, he's really not suffering. You have to go on with your taking care of yourself.

speaker: interviewee
text: You cannot stop and feeling sorry for anybody, because feeling sorry is not making it better for the person, but it's making it worse for you. She was trying to explain to a 12, 13-year-old how to live under these circumstances. I listened to her, but it-- it just didn't make sense. And then finally, we got to the fourth experiment-- the third experiment, which was they selected the five of us and they-- they-- again, with the smooth skin-- and they said that we're going to another camp, but we will be back. And they said that we will go.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she found out from the girl in charge that they're taking us to Majdanek. And-- and she says, well, what is Majdanek? And she said, well, I hate to tell you this, but I think that Ilse Koch, that was taking the-- the human skin and making gloves and lampshades in, I think, her-- in the camp she was doing that was Innsbruck. I don't remember the camp she was in. That she was coming to Majdanek to select her-- her-- her material for the lampshades, and we have the skin for it. And she said to me, let's go back to the barrack and not go-- not go here. And I said, well, are they going let us do that? She says no, they're going to kill us, but it's better to die here than going to Majdanek and let them skin our skin. I says, they're going to skin our skin? Why? And she said, because there is an-- there is an SS woman, and she is a maniac. She has to be a maniac to do this. She likes human gloves made out of human skin, and lampshades, and God knows what else she-- she does.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I says, oh, I'm not going. Let's go back to the barrack. But of course we never got back to the barrack. And then they never took us to Ilse Koch. They took us, but we never saw anybody. So I guess either it was a scare for us, or she couldn't make it on that day. So we were--

speaker: interviewer
text: They took you to Majdanek.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah, and they took us back. We walked, because I remembered Majdanek. We went-- we walked from the city-- the five of us, with two guards-- into the camp. They put us in a barrack. And we stayed there, and the next day, we walked back, and they took us back again. See, they needed us for another experiment. And then when we came back, they-- they-- they-- we were in our barrack for a few days, I think, or mostly a week. And then they took us into the [? revere. ?] And I don't know. I don't remember whether it was the [? revere ?] in Birkenau or the [? revere ?] in Auschwitz But they were-- they were going-- they told us that they were going to take the number out of our arm. And Sabka and I said, oh, well, that'll be good. At least if we survive, we won't have a reminder that we were here. And she says, if we survive, and that's all she said. And then they took us in, and we didn't go back to the barrack after, but they kept us there. And they-- they never gave us an anesthetic. And there were just the two of us out of the five. So, again, I don't know what happened to the other three. I don't know whether they died or they-- they took them somewheres else. But now, there was only the two of us left. And we were laying on a-- on a rusty table alongside one another, and the doctor and the nurse were between the two of us. And they were-- first, they were injecting things into our arm, like underneath the-- the-- and that wasn't too bad, because it was just like an injection. And then they were pulling things out of there. And by then, our arm was swelling and-- and then they were-- and then they were cutting without-- anesthetic. And-- and within a week, I think, or 10 days, they found a way to get rid of the number.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we didn't know why. Why would they want to take it out after they put it in, and why us? Why do they want our number out? And then the nurse that was working with them, she told us that the SS have SS printed under their left arm. And now they want their identity-- they want to hide their identity. They don't want that in their body. So they are experimenting on our numbers, because they have the same ink in the SS sign under their arm as they used for our numbers. So they used our bodies for-- to see how fast they can get that out so they can get it out of the SS. How long were you on the table being experimented on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hours at a time. And then they would just let us be there, bleeding, and then, thank God for that nurse. She used to, like, come. Like, when she used to like, write things down that they told her to write, she-- and if we were worked on, she would, like, one arm, whoever she was closer. She would hold that spot really tight, so you know, I guess to stop the blood from--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they work on the whole number at once, or small sections--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they took the section underneath the number, the whole-- the whole section. They did. They worked together, because they were injecting, and then they were pulling, and then they were cutting. So I don't know. You know, I don't know. Everything was-- was tampered with, all of the numbers.

speaker: interviewer
text: And no sedative, no anaesthetic?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, nothing. Half of the times we were, like, out. And when we came to, we were either bleeding or they were still digging or-- or-- and then the final-- when they-- when they found what they were looking for, they ordered the nurse to give us the-- an injection. And I said, oh good, maybe the pain will go away. But it wasn't the injection for the pain to go away. It was supposed to be the lethal injection. And yes, she did give us an injection. And we did sort of got woozy and all of that, and then she filled out all kind of papers. And she handed to them, and-- and she yanked us off the table, like this, real fast, but they accepted her paper. And she, like, kind of ran with us and threw us into this room. And there were people there, sitting on the floor. I mean, they were, like, half dead. And she says, don't go near those people, just sit there. And on the way-- on the way in there, before she closed the door, she says, and just remember, never remember your number that they just took out, and never remember your name, or you are dead. And she threw us in that room and she closed the door. And she says, if I can, I will be back, and she left. And I guess they accepted her-- her-- you know, that we are-- we were put in there to go to the gas chamber. And she filled out the papers that we are dead. And we're gone, and our numbers are taken away, and everything was-- and they accepted it. When they left, she came into the room. She took us out of there. And she put us in a-- she took us into a barrack in camp that we weren't there before. So in case there are people that remember us, they shouldn't say, you know, that we were there before. So she gave us a different number, a different name, and a different barrack.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the two of us survived this experiment and went to-- with her help. Then, I never knew who that nurse was until the day before liberation. My friend Sabka told me how-- why she did that and-- and that she-- she helped us to live. Until the day-- the day before liberation, I didn't know none of this. I just thought that was the part of the procedure for this experiment. And after she put us into this barrack, the next [INAUDIBLE] appell, my-- my friend Sabka was taken away, and again I was left alone in a barrack with strangers.
